text: Welcome to another episode of robotic hernia surgery. Today we're talking about robotic preperitoneal repair for a flank hernia. I find off midline flank hernias and atypical hernias to be some of the best ways to apply robotic technology. This was a seventy-seven-year-old woman with a history of laparoscopic left adrenalectomy who presented with an incisional flank hernia. We had optimized her with regard to diabetes.

Her obesity was noted and we moved forward with our plan for a hernia repair. Of note, her past surgical history had highlighted dense adhesions and so we and mentally prepared for potential need for robotic adhesiolysis. Hernia dimensions were three by four centimeters, EHSL-II. It was an interparietal hernia, involved small bowel and colon, but the external oblique was still intact.

In terms of surgical planning, my goal usually in these cases is to dock on the contralateral side. Upon entry, we had a wide segment of the midline, which had dense adhesions. And so as a result, we did end up docking much closer to the medial aspect of the hernia, probably about twelve to thirteen centimeters from the hernia defect, closer than I like.

The surgeon observes epiploic fat from the colon involved in the hernia and identifies the small bowel. The surgical field shows the sidewall where the bowel has not been completely mobilized away from the hernia. The surgeon prepares to develop the flap for preperitoneal or retromuscular dissection.

The surgeon transitions between preperitoneal and pre-transversalis dissection, adjusting based on tissue condition. The pre-transversalis dissection appears slightly bloody, prompting a switch to preperitoneal. Cephalad to the hernia, the peritoneum thins, leading to a quick transition to pre-transversalis retromuscular dissection.

The surgeon develops the space by dissecting a combination of transversus abdominis and diaphragm fat, moving infrattened cephalad towards the hernia margins. The hernia sac is incised, and dissection proceeds around the neck of the hernia, ensuring safe closure of the posterior aspect.

The surgeon dissects diaphragmatic fibers away from the transversalis fascia and peritoneum, creating a space on the opposite side of the hernia. The dissection proceeds caudad, aiming to unify the plane, transitioning between preperitoneal and retromuscular pre-transversalis planes, with a straightforward dissection observed.

The surgeon identifies the volcano sign and begins reducing the hernia sac with minimal bowel manipulation. The hernia is a true intraparietal type, with anterior muscle fibers visible, likely from the internal or external oblique. Circumferential dissection around the hernia is completed, and the surgeon proceeds to take down the remaining sac, including some muscle tissue. The dissection occurs in a subdiaphragmatic plane, preparing to close the defect and reinforce the abdominal wall with a large mesh.

The surgeon performs a circumferential dissection around the hernia defect, revealing the bony prominences of the pelvis. The defect closure begins using a number one non-absorbable V-lock suture, placating the defect closed along with anterior tissue. No drain is placed to minimize the potential for interparietal seroma.

The surgeon performs sequential closure of the hernia defect, tightening the sutures to re-approximate the edges of the transversus abdominis and diaphragm muscles. The suture is run back over itself to lock it and further plicate the abdominal wall and surrounding healthy tissue.

The surgeon measures the lateral sidewall hernia space using a ruler, determining it to be approximately seventeen by twenty centimeters. A macroporous polypropylene mesh is cut to fit the measured space. The mesh is oriented and trimmed to size, and a standard peritoneal flap closure is performed, closing the defect where the hernia sac was abandoned.

So this portion of the case went great, and the patient did very well. This was an outpatient operation. I checked in with the patient a few days after. They used no narcotics in their recovery phase and were doing a lot better with regard to their symptoms. Thank you very much.
